chapters.title: You can live a creative and a prosperous life
chapters.text:
Michelangelo, the designer of the Sistine Chapel during the Renaissance era, has been misconstrued as a starving artist and grouped with the likes of Vincent van Gogh. But an investigation of bank records by Harvard Professor Rab Hatfield revealed otherwise. Michelangelo lived a frugal life but was extremely wealthy.

Henri Murger popularized the conception of artists as poor in his collection of stories titled “Scenes.” Murger was the son of a concierge and a tailor in France who grew frustrated that creative geniuses couldn’t achieve financial security. He painted artists as impoverished people who could not make money from their creative works. Today, we warn people who appear creative to contain their creativity to avoid becoming starving artists.

The story of the starving artist is not true, and we must change our paradigm to reflect on it.

Mediocrity is chosen over excellence because humans desire the path of least resistance. We are risk averse. Thus, we avoid struggle, ignore our passion, and choose the popular route instead.

In the following chapters, we will discover how to change this narrative. You can make a living as an artist. You can approach your work as art so that you unleash your creativity and inspire the next generation to do the same. Michelangelo's transformative influence shifted the perception of artists, elevating them from mere laborers to esteemed figures within society. Today, you have the opportunity to join the New Renaissance by studying the triumphs of accomplished creatives. Remember, each of us possesses a creative spark, and being taken seriously as an artist involves embracing innovative thinking and lifestyle choices rather than adhering to the cliché of struggling for recognition.

Did you know? Michelangelo's worth is equivalent to $47 million today.

chapters.title: Thriving artists live by the principles of the New Renaissance
chapters.text:
The New Renaissance refers to contemporary professionals who are doing creative work and thriving. Principles are universal and can be applied to specific contexts. The world works on principles, and successful people understand these natural laws and follow them. There are 12 principles that successful artists live by. They are:

• Flourishing artists are made, not born.
• Prosperous artists steal ideas from their environment instead of striving to be original.
• Successful artists have the mindset of growth instead of thinking they have enough talent.
• Triumphant artists are flexible but stubborn about the right things.
• Thriving artists put themselves in the limelight and attract clients’ attention.
• Palmy artists insert themselves in a community of creatives.
• Flourishing artists believe in collaboration.
• Successful artists work in the full glare of the world.
• Prosperous artists ensure they get paid for what they do.
• Thriving artists protect the ownership of their work.
• Palmy artists acquire multiple skills instead of focusing on one craft.
• Triumphant artists make money to make art.

These principles can be collapsed into three themes that surround the work of thriving artists, namely mindset, market, and money.

Every meaningful life operates on specific principles that guide thought and action.

To thrive, an artist must be intentional about networking with the right people who will draw the world’s attention to their work. Attention is meaningful only if it earns you money.

chapters.title: Changing beliefs about creative work
chapters.text:
Deciding to follow one’s dream may become scary because we’ve come too far to quit. You are what you are because of your decisions.

Self-discovery is essential to discovering your dream. Let go of the past and embrace your new story by creating it. It is not too late to become the person you wish to be, even if the cost is taking up a new identity. It is hard to make difficult choices since our new choice will drill us into becoming outstanding people. Of course, it won't be easy, but it will be worth it.

We break rules to set rules.

Creativity flourishes when we dare to challenge conventions and carve out novel trajectories. Professor Paul Torrance, a renowned psychologist, delved into the relationship between unconventional behavior and creative aptitude. Through his studies, he unearthed that individuals with a creative inclination often grapple within a system that makes them follow rules they don’t understand. Outstanding creative work cannot be attained by following rules. Therefore, it’s important to break those rules.

The only way to break the rules and set another is by recreating yourself with the possibilities and imaginations to attain transformation.

Before you can create great art, you first have to create yourself. ~ Jeff Goins

Your beliefs determine who you are. They come from what people think of you, how they treat you, and how they see themselves. Abandon received identity. Embrace the power to take small risks as you strive to become a thriving artist. As we take each step, new challenges will surface, but we need to remind ourselves that we are never done becoming who we want to be.

Great artists never do the same thing repeatedly, expecting different results. They reinvest in themselves and continue to build on who they are and what they have done.

Reinventing oneself allows one to do better work than before and be the best at it.

It is better to leave where we are, even if we are scared of deciding, than to stay there seeking a means to an end. We need to erase the starving artist mentality by taking small steps as we become a truer version of ourselves.

chapters.title: Five major steps for optimum creativity
chapters.text:
The ability to pull old ideas together to gain new insight shows that we are great artists because original works don’t exist. Great artists copy the works of their masters and peers until they can create their own style. They do not try to be original. The rule remains the same for us today: we must study and consistently practice our studies. We can create and build our reputations with humility and the spirit of students, although it requires great effort.

You can become a thriving artist by learning, being patient, humble, and persevering.

Thriving artists are ready to do what ordinary artists won’t do to become extraordinary. We can be the best at what we do if we seek help, embrace invaluable training that doesn’t come on a platter of gold, and devote our time to consistent practice. Starving artists work on their own, but thriving artists seek help because they believe that great work is not a result of luck but a willingness to learn and be the best.

Be stubborn with your vision to succeed.

Stubbornness is the willingness to keep going even when the road is rough. It is an asset in creative work. Stubbornness helps us to achieve greater things that seem unachievable. It will help us not only to start but to see things through. It is a liability that becomes an asset as it matures.

Tenacity can trigger our excellence with the energy to create commendable work.

chapters.title: Tackling the market
chapters.text:
The market is an avenue to sell your creativity. It opens your eyes to the real world. We network and advertise our talents to the public while we earn. The power of influencers is needed to market our services. These people can make or mar your product.

Excellent work will be appreciated if an influencer vouches for it to people who don't know it exists.

You need someone who acknowledges your potential and believes in your creativity. You must have convinced the influencer repeatedly to believe in your work. Influencers will not look for you. You need to find them and establish a reliable relationship with them. Influencers are everywhere, but be patient, ready, and eager to learn.

Influencers are closer to us than we think. They may not live a life of affluence or be impactful leaders. Common people who are ready to promote our work with the resources they have can be our influencers.

Thriving artists believe they are teachable; they are willing to learn, unlike starving artists, who believe that they know it all even while starving. The choice is ours to make, but we should follow the steps of the thriving artist. Influencers always support teachable artists; the only way to get an influencer is by learning from those who come our way. We all need someone to rely on. We can’t succeed alone. We should make use of every opportunity that comes our way.

Don't rely on what you know; reach out to experts who can transform your creative work.

Thriving artists go the extra mile to learn from others and become extraordinary, while striving artists remain static and choose to be ordinary. The extra mile can be any productive effort ranging from changing location to doing extra work or rendering free services.

It is said that people and places determine the gravity of our success more than we know.

Location is essential to creativity. You will be happy to live in a place where your profession and your lifestyle are encouraged.

chapters.title: Collaboration is vital to success as a creative
chapters.text:
We need a force that will keep pushing us to success. But these forces can only come from making connections with people who are eager to see you succeed.

You need to build a network to join the thriving artist community.

Creative work cannot be successful without the right network. Skill and talent may not be enough to grant us success. Skill will make you stand in front of the right people, but it cannot magnify your reach. You don’t need a battalion to create your network.

Great work does not come about through a single stroke of genius, but by the continual effort of a community. ~ Jeff Goins

Sometimes, the community we desire is around us, but it will not find us unless we move out of our comfort zone and meet like minds that will help us thrive.

Proving your worth will accelerate your success. You prove your worth by rendering services any way you can. It is easy to succeed if you join groups that support your creativity and share your work.

Success depends on collaborating with like minds.

Creativity cannot thrive in isolation. It will succeed best if other like-minded minds are involved in the work. We need people to help us when we are down or feeling discouraged. Creative breakthroughs in history resulted from small groups of people working together, not individual inventions.

Collaborations are driven by competition. Break away from the expected and compete with experts to furnish your ideas. All art needs healthy competition to make the creator an expert. We need to channel our energy towards learning from the masters, and we will become one of them with time. Instead of being jealous of others' creativity and feeling threatened by it, you should channel that emotion into creating better work with the mindset of becoming a master. Competition will help us keep sharpening our work and continue growing as artists. Leading, managing, and collaborating with peers are essential to successful artists.

Sometimes, we need more than our peers, team, and co-workers. We may need a professional to assist us with our work. We need to remember that there is strength in numbers when the ambitions align.

chapters.title: Sharing one’s work is essential
chapters.text:
Promotion is an essential part of your job. Starving artists believe that the world can find them wherever they are because they have done an excellent job. Sadly, that’s not true. Your work needs to be in front of the people who will react to it because they will not care about what you do if they cannot see it.

The thriving artist will share their work with the public and give room for criticism.

It is essential to share our work to attract attention, improve, and be more confident. People will repay our effort with generosity if we showcase our work. Do what thriving artists do by putting your work where it has the greatest potential to succeed.

Thriving artists risk rejection because they want to be discovered. We will encounter failure and disappointments, but we can decide to give in to them or see them as an avenue for improvement. We need to see failures as “training camps” since they are unavoidable. They are necessary roads to great success.

Adequate practice distinguishes a thriving artist from a striving artist. Thriving artists get to work immediately, while a striving artist waits for a conducive atmosphere to start working. For us to be exceptional at what we do, we need to start early practice because the more preparation we have, the better we will be. Cartoonist Stephanie Halligan drew a cartoon every day for two years without fail. She understood that practice would improve her, and her audience would see her progress. We can showcase our work to the world by hyping it and practicing in public. They will repay us with their loyalty.

chapters.title: Mastering the efficacy of money accelerates productivity
chapters.text:
Thriving artists know that their art cannot survive without money, while starving artists avoid it at all costs. Starving artists continue to starve because they work for free. Money is not the reason for doing what we like, but it’s part of the road we need to walk to become professional.

We can only get the fuel to keep working if we charge for the services we are rendering to the world. It brings dignity to our work. Some people offer free services when starting out with the hope of building a portfolio, but it is not the way to start a career. Contrary to the notion that working without pay opens doors to opportunity, a recent three-year analysis by the National Association of Colleges and Employers reveals that unpaid internships place graduates at a distinct disadvantage. Among 9200 students surveyed, 63.1% with paid internships received at least one job offer, while merely 37% of those with unpaid internships secured offers with lower compensation.

We often think that working for free attracts opportunity. But opportunities don’t put food on the table or pay off student loans. Stop offering free services if you want to stop starving. Free services devalue your work and determine how people treat you. What people say doesn’t matter. What counts is taking yourself seriously so that others can do the same. The soul of business can only survive with money.

Although some artists don't care about money — all they want is to create — they need to understand that charging is not about compensation; it is about dignity. Free work leaves a feeling of frustration and bitterness in the artist.

Cultivate the habit of owning and protecting your work to remain in control.

A long-term goal is a prerequisite to long-term success, and we cannot be in control by settling for short-term gain. Our desire to have a lasting and prosperous career can be possible by owning our work. Thriving artists are patient with their work. They don't sell to early bidders, which will make them lose control of their work. They hold out and own their work. Ownership buys you freedom.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The goal of every artist is to thrive and not starve. But this is only possible with the right mindset, training, exposure, and reward. There will be times when your mind wanders. You need to focus on larger ideas and think about the body of work you are creating instead of a single creation. You will focus on important things if you cultivate a portfolio mindset.

Furthermore, you can transform your wandering mind into diverse sets of interests and skills that can coalesce into a substantial body of work. Embrace distraction as a potential catalyst that amplifies creativity and fuels innovative thinking. Opportunities come with an open mind, and our creative tricks can help us identify those opportunities.

Procrastination is an enemy of creativity. You will learn new skills and leverage existing ones only if you are able to crush procrastination. The road to success is a process. Success doesn’t happen suddenly. We should focus on our goal, design a portfolio that’s attractive, and create the world we want.

Technology is one of the tools to accelerate our success, and it is important we dive into and make good use of it. Gone are the days when people queued for everything. We can achieve anything we want to achieve without having to wait for others. We need to push the boundaries of possibilities. Our gifts and technology will yield undeniable success if we take advantage of them.

We don’t have to starve if we choose the part of a thriving artist and experience transformation. We can create work that matters and earn while doing it.

Try this


• Meet like-minded people and learn from them.
• Expand your network.
• Ensure constant practice and get feedback on your work.
• Do not be afraid to charge a fee for your services.
